title: Random Access Code protocols: Quantum advantage related to intraparticle entanglement-based contextuality
authors: Nilaj Saha, Sumit Mukherjee, Dipankar Home
abstract: The quantum enhancement of success probability in the Random Access Code (RAC) protocols remains unexplored from two important perspectives. First, the use of entanglement between two co-measurable degrees of freedom of a single particle (intraparticle entanglement) in achieving such quantum enhancement has not been investigated. Second, no explicit quantitative correspondence has been established between the predicted/observed quantum advantage and the underlying quantum resource responsible for it. In this work, we address both these aspects simultaneously by harnessing a single-particle resource. For this purpose, the RAC protocol is formulated in terms of intraparticle entanglement between, for instance, spin/polarization and path degrees of freedom of a single particle. Within this framework, a relevant Bell-type inequality, derived from the assumption of noncontextuality for single particle path-spin measurements, is used. Based on these ingredients, the formulated analysis reveals that the magnitude of quantum-mechanical violation of such Bell-type inequality, signifying a form of quantum contextuality, is quantitatively commensurate with the quantum enhancement of success probability in any intraparticle entanglement-assisted $n$-bit RAC protocol. In particular, the maximal success probability of a quantum $n \mapsto 1$ RAC protocol corresponds to the maximal quantum violation of the relevant Bell-type inequality. This correspondence is empirically testable using a readily implementable single-particle interferometric setup requiring coherence preservation only for a single particle.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the increase in success probability in quantum Random Access Code protocols is quantitatively tied to the violation of single-particle noncontextuality inequalities. By using intraparticle entanglement between path and spin, the authors show that the quantum advantage scales directly with the strength of the underlying quantum resource. This establishes a measurable, fundamental connection between communication complexity and quantum contextuality.
main_problem: The paper aims to establish a quantitative correspondence between the quantum advantage in Random Access Code (RAC) protocols and the magnitude of intraparticle entanglement-based contextuality violations.
main_result: The authors prove that the enhancement in success probability for n-to-1 RAC protocols is directly commensurate with the violation of single-particle noncontextuality Bell-type inequalities, providing a universal relation between success probability and the Bell-type parameter.
method: The study uses an algebraic isomorphism between path and spin Hilbert spaces to map RAC protocols onto Bell-CHSH-type inequalities, employing a concatenation scheme to generalize results from 2-to-1 and 3-to-1 subunits to n-to-1 protocols.
model_or_system: The physical model involves single-particle systems utilizing intraparticle entanglement between co-measurable degrees of freedom, such as path and spin/polarization, implementable via Mach-Zehnder interferometry or photonic setups.
key_observables: Success probability (P), Bell-type correlation variables (C_n), and detector counts in interferometric setups.
important_parameters: n-bit encoding (n-to-1), quantum advantage (delta P), and the magnitude of noncontextuality violation (beta).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the validity of noncontextuality for single-particle path-spin measurements and the mathematical equivalence between intraparticle and interparticle entanglement descriptions.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows schematic diagrams of 2-to-1 and 3-to-1 RAC subunits and examples of 4-to-1 and 5-to-1 protocol concatenations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of RAC enhancement, establishes the classical bounds using noncontextuality, develops the quantum framework using intraparticle entanglement, provides a mathematical mapping via Hilbert space isomorphism, generalizes the result to n-bit protocols via concatenation, and discusses experimental feasibility.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between information-theoretic tasks (RAC) and quantum foundations (contextuality), showing how single-particle resources can be used to quantify quantum advantage, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Mach-Zehnder arrangement for the scheme: Spin-polarized neutrons passing through beamsplitters (BS1 & BS2+PS2) are prepared in the intraparticle entangled state of Eq. (12), followed by spin measurements using two Stern-Gerlach devices (SG1 & SG2). Appropriate settings (θ, ϕ) of BS2+PS2 and SG orientations enable joint measurements of path ( ˆAi) and spin ( ˆBj). Detector counts are recorded such that Alice at D3,4 measures path (via combined channels) and Bob at D′ 3,4, D′′ 3,4 measures spin.
FIG. 2: Schematic representation of the concatenation scheme for EARACs

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper's central mechanism uses intraparticle entanglement between internal (spin) and external (path) degrees of freedom to establish a link between contextuality and quantum advantage.
